Last summer, I went to Ashoka University for a one-month summer program, and it had a big impact on me...

Last summer, I spent a month living at Ashoka University in Sonipat, Haryana, as part of the Lodha Genius Programme. When I first arrived, dragging my suitcase across the campus and into a dorm room that smelled faintly of detergent and unfamiliarity, it didn’t fully register what I was doing. I had never lived away from home for that long. Everything felt unfamiliar—the room, the people, even the silence at night. I still remember thinking, this is really it. I’m on my own now. For one whole month.

The programme itself was built around maths and science classes, but what stayed with me had very little to do with classrooms. What I remember most is the feeling of suddenly having complete ownership over my time. After classes ended, there was no fixed structure waiting for me. Some days, I’d go straight to the cricket nets, still in my classroom clothes, and bat until the sky turned orange and the floodlights flickered on. Other evenings, a group of us would just sit outside in the gardens, talking for hours about all sorts of things. Even with the 10 p.m. curfew and the campus boundaries, it still felt like more freedom than I had ever experienced. That's when I realized that freedom wasn't about having no limits, rather it was about having ownership over the time between them. And at Ashoka, I had a lot of ownership over my time.

At first, I did feel homesick. There were moments, especially at night, when everything slowed down and I became aware of how far away I was from everything familiar. I remember how, when I was younger, my dad used to travel frequently for work, moving between cities and hotel rooms every week. I never thought much about it then. But lying in that unfamiliar dorm room, I finally understood what that must have felt like — the quiet unfamiliarity, the need to rebuild a sense of normal in a place that wasn’t yours yet.

However, that feeling didn’t last. Without noticing exactly when it happened, the campus started to feel comfortable. The routines became mine and things felt more normal. That's when I stopped noticing how new everything was and started just living in it. And at some point, I stopped counting how many days were left till I could return home and actually started wishing there were more. 

During the first week, each day felt so long and unfamiliar, and at the end, I felt like I had been living in Ashoka for a month. But as I settled in, time seemed to speed up and the last two weeks literally flew by. This made me realize how strongly our sense of time is tied to how comfortable we feel. When everything is unfamiliar, we notice every moment, but when it becomes home, the moments blend together.

Sharing a room with someone I had never met before was part of that change. In the beginning, it felt slightly awkward — two strangers trying to coexist in the same small space. But over time, it stopped feeling like we were strangers at all. That was true for so many people there. When you eat together every day, walk to class together, play sports together, and exist in the same space constantly, something shifts. I made friends with people my age all over India, from Kashmir to Delhi to Hyderabad, and quite a few from Mumbai too.

There was also a quiet shift in how I saw myself. At home, my days had always been structured for me, with school, homework, and routines that ran almost automatically. But over there, nothing happened unless I chose it. Initially, that felt quite strange, and honestly, there was a sense of lingering emptiness. But gradually, I stopped waiting for structure and started creating it. That was when I realized independence isn’t something that suddenly appears—it grows quietly, through small decisions you make every day.

As a result, a quiet confidence started to grow within me. Small things, like deciding how to spend my time, managing my own schedule, or choosing whether to study, rest, or go out, instead of moving on autopilot between school bells and routines in Mumbai, gave me the experience of structuring my own life. And more importantly, I realized I liked that responsibility and control.

Of course, the academic side was interesting. We explored ideas like investing, cryptography, microscopy, and how architecture shapes emotion in films. The classes were different from school—they felt more open, more driven by curiosity than by exams. But when I think back, the academics feel almost secondary to everything else I experienced outside the classroom. What stays with me most are the unplanned moments. Sitting on the grass after sunset, watching the campus slowly empty. Walking back to the dorms late at night, when everything was quiet except for the sound of footsteps and distant conversations. Playing sports until my legs hurt.

At some point, without realizing it, the campus stopped feeling temporary. My dorm room felt normal. The walk to the dining hall felt routine. The people around me felt like part of my everyday life. It was so strange to think that just weeks earlier, all of it had felt unfamiliar.

Overall, I realized I wasn’t someone who needed familiarity to feel comfortable. I could build that comfort anywhere. I also saw that independence wasn’t something to fear but rather something I naturally grew into. By the end, LGP felt less like a temporary programme and more like a glimpse into an independent future.

Eventually, when I left, I wasn’t the same person who had arrived with that suitcase a month earlier. The campus that once felt unfamiliar had become a place I didn’t want to leave. For the first time, I had experienced what it meant to live independently — not just physically, but mentally. I returned home with more confidence, stronger friendships, and the realization that I was ready for independence and excited for it.

When I arrived, I remember that anxious thought... I’m on my own now.
When I left, that thought no longer felt intimidating. It felt natural.

In Gameweek 23, Arsenal beat relegation-battling Wolves at the Molineux, securing three points and a clean sheet with a 1-0 win. Finally, we won with 10 men. This keeps the gap between us and Liverpool level as they enter a double gameweek against Bournemouth and Everton. Controversial Red Card Initially, we weren't attacking with enough intensity. Two minutes before halftime, Myles Lewis-Skelly clipped a Wolves player who was about to launch a counterattack near their box. Michael Oliver deemed it a "serious challenge" and sent the 18-year-old off, a decision many fans and pundits felt only warranted a yellow. VAR chose not to intervene beyond confirming the decision. We were forced to play the remainder of the match with 10 men. After the restart, Oliver did not give Gomes a second yellow for kicking the ball away. Later, instead of awarding a straight red for a harsher challenge on Timber, he issued Gomes a second yellow. Substitute Calafiori capitalized soon after, scoring the decisive goal minutes following the equalizer in numbers. We ultimately won the 10v10 battle. Defensive Resilience Despite the numerical disadvantage, our defence held firm. Wolves created a few chances after the hour mark, but Raya and the backline remained composed. We secured our second consecutive clean sheet. Fun fact: the last time we won back-to-back games with back-to-back clean sheets was our opening fixtures against Wolves and Villa in August. Defensive solidity has not been as consistent this season as last. Midfield Performance Partey delivered a smooth display, and Rice continued his strong form following his first Champions League goal. Nwaneri had a decent outing filling in for Odegaard, who missed the game due to illness. Though naturally more suited to the right wing, he performed adequately in midfield. Attacking Concerns Martinelli was arguably our best forward despite playing on the right instead of his preferred left flank. Trossard was less creative than in recent games but still serviceable. Havertz struggled again, missing two key first-half chances and reinforcing concerns about our lack of a clinical striker. Super-Sub Impact Calafiori came on at halftime and scored with a composed left-footed volley. It was his second Arsenal goal, the first being a stunning strike against Manchester City. His contribution secured a crucial victory. Looking Ahead Next, we host Manchester City. Despite their struggles and recent defeat to PSG, players like Foden and new signing Marmoush remain threats. City are improving and will test us. Hopefully, our appeal for the Lewis-Skelly red card succeeds so he can feature against City. He has been exceptional at left back and would be a valuable option, whether starting or off the bench. That's all for today. If you enjoyed the post, please share it and leave your thoughts below. Thanks for reading. -- Om Jhamvar

In Gameweek 23, Arsenal beat relegation-battling Wolves at the Molineux, securing three points and a clean sheet with a 1-0 win. Finally, we won with 10 men. This keeps the gap between us and Liverpool level as they enter a double gameweek against Bournemouth and Everton.

Controversial Red Card

Initially, we weren’t attacking with enough intensity. Two minutes before halftime, Myles Lewis-Skelly clipped a Wolves player who was about to launch a counterattack near their box. Michael Oliver deemed it a “serious challenge” and sent the 18-year-old off, a decision many fans and pundits felt only warranted a yellow. VAR chose not to intervene beyond confirming the decision.

We were forced to play the remainder of the match with 10 men.

After the restart, Oliver did not give Gomes a second yellow for kicking the ball away. Later, instead of awarding a straight red for a harsher challenge on Timber, he issued Gomes a second yellow. Substitute Calafiori capitalized soon after, scoring the decisive goal minutes following the equalizer in numbers. We ultimately won the 10v10 battle.

Defensive Resilience

Despite the numerical disadvantage, our defence held firm. Wolves created a few chances after the hour mark, but Raya and the backline remained composed. We secured our second consecutive clean sheet.

Fun fact: the last time we won back-to-back games with back-to-back clean sheets was our opening fixtures against Wolves and Villa in August. Defensive solidity has not been as consistent this season as last.

Midfield Performance

Partey delivered a smooth display, and Rice continued his strong form following his first Champions League goal. Nwaneri had a decent outing filling in for Odegaard, who missed the game due to illness. Though naturally more suited to the right wing, he performed adequately in midfield.

Attacking Concerns

Martinelli was arguably our best forward despite playing on the right instead of his preferred left flank. Trossard was less creative than in recent games but still serviceable. Havertz struggled again, missing two key first-half chances and reinforcing concerns about our lack of a clinical striker.

Super-Sub Impact

Calafiori came on at halftime and scored with a composed left-footed volley. It was his second Arsenal goal, the first being a stunning strike against Manchester City. His contribution secured a crucial victory.

Looking Ahead

Next, we host Manchester City. Despite their struggles and recent defeat to PSG, players like Foden and new signing Marmoush remain threats. City are improving and will test us.

Hopefully, our appeal for the Lewis-Skelly red card succeeds so he can feature against City. He has been exceptional at left back and would be a valuable option, whether starting or off the bench.

That’s all for today. If you enjoyed the post, please share it and leave your thoughts below.

Thanks for reading.

FIVE STAR WIN AGAINST MAN CITY!
A deep dive into the match that could define the title race...

WE BEAT MANCHESTER CITY 5-1! I cannot believe it! I don't think even the most optimistic Gunner would have predicted this, but what a game that was! That was an absolute demolition job as we beat the reigning Premier League champions by four goals at the Emirates. I'm so pumped, I don't know where to start! The atmosphere at the Emirates during this game was electric. It just goes to show how massive of a victory this was.

And the entertainment... Gabriel screaming in Haaland's face, tugging his shorts and refusing to shake his hand. Then there were the 'Stay Humble Eh' banners displayed by the Arsenal fans, although the award of this week's 'Stay Humble Eh' moment surely goes to Myles Lewis Skelly and his cheeky Haaland celebration.

We started with a bang, capitalizing on Akanji's mistake as Rice's astute touch forward allowed Odegaard to open the scoring in just a minute and forty-five seconds. However, after getting the lead, we sat back and, in my opinion, gave Manchester City much more possession than they deserved. Gvardiol nearly scored from a corner, but luckily Haaland couldn't reach it in time.

Havertz missed an opportunity to double our lead with an open goal as he fired wide. Granted, he redeemed himself later with a goal to complement his opening assist. We finished the first half one goal up.

The rivalry between the two sides was palpable, especially given last season's tension after Haaland threw the ball at Gabriel during the 2-2 draw. However, nothing too dramatic happened in the first half... yet.

SECOND HALF

Ten minutes after the restart, Haaland equalized with a header and it briefly felt like City were back. Arsenal had gone into defensive mode and paid for it. But just 105 seconds later, a miscued pass from Foden and a deflected strike from Partey off Stones restored our lead.

This time, we did not retreat. A fabulous goal from Skelly gave us breathing room, and from there it was all Arsenal. Havertz scored the fourth on a swift counterattack, and Nwaneri came off the bench to score a stunning fifth in stoppage time. It was the icing on the cake of what I believe was our best match of the season.

PROS:

1. Myles Lewis Skelly was sensational with his dribbling, defending, and sublime curler, not to mention his composed celebration. The way he shut down Foden and Marmoush showed unbelievable maturity for an 18-year-old. He played like a seasoned full back and demonstrated remarkable confidence. To me, he deserved Player of the Match.

2. Ethan Nwaneri was outstanding off the bench. The 17-year-old played with confidence and capped it off with a beautiful finesse finish. Another Hale End talent proving the academy's quality.

3. Partey, Odegaard, and Rice dominated the midfield with progressive runs and control. The midfield battle was completely in our favor.

4. The defense was rock-solid. Our center-halves kept Haaland quiet, Timber was solid on the right, and Raya was dependable in goal.

5. Our tactical high press disrupted City repeatedly and led to crucial opportunities.

6. After conceding, we responded with intensity and refused to slow down. We stayed relentless and finished the game properly.

NEGATIVES:

1. We were not clinical enough early on. We often score first and then slow the tempo instead of pushing for dominance. That nearly cost us in past matches.

2. Havertz is not a natural striker. While he scored and assisted, he lacks the instinct of a true No. 9. We still need a proper striker if we want to win major trophies.

All in all, this was more than a win. It was the culmination of a three-year battle with City. We can finally say we are better than them this season. Now we must build on this momentum and push forward.

That's all for today. I know I wrote a lot, but defining victories like these deserves passion. Thanks for reading!

-- Om Jhamvar

NEW FALSE NINE?? MERINO SCORES BRACE!
Arsenal beat Leicester City 2-0 away, with Merino scoring...

Arsenal beat Leicester City 2-0 away, securing a clean sheet and moving to within 4 points of Liverpool, who still have a game in hand. This came after devastating news of Havertz's hamstring injury ruling him out for the rest of the season. It was a massive result, and though we didn't play brilliantly, super-sub Merino scored both goals to make the difference. Defense Should Have Been Better As usual, we dominated possession and kept a clean sheet. However, our defence was not as solid as expected against a 19th-placed side that hadn't scored in their previous four home matches. Leicester created a few clear chances that could have changed the game. For a title-contending team facing relegation opposition, more control was needed. Odegaard Must Step Up Declan Rice was our best midfielder and tried to compensate for Havertz's absence with forward runs. He wasn't effective as a makeshift striker but contributed well overall. Odegaard showed flashes of creativity but must now take responsibility in front of goal, especially without Saka. Partey looked fatigued and was rightly substituted for Jorginho. Merino Delivers Nwaneri was electric, constantly troubling Leicester's defence. Trossard improved after shifting left, though he struggled initially as a striker. Sterling offered very little and was replaced by Merino, whose two goals prevented what felt like another frustrating draw. Can Merino Be the False Nine? This was only one of the first times Merino has played as a striker, making his impact even more impressive. With only three fit forwards available and Sterling out of form, options are limited. Merino's physical presence at 6ft 2in and strong aerial ability could make him effective in that role, particularly from crosses. Trossard remains better suited to the left wing, and Nwaneri thrives on the right, where his creativity drives our attacks. Moving them centrally would reduce attacking balance. That leaves Merino as the most viable solution for now. He displayed more confidence and clinical instinct than Havertz has shown recently, especially with his header and composed finish. While he has spent most of his career as a midfielder, this unconventional adjustment could prove beneficial. Conclusion I'm not entirely convinced this is the long-term answer, but it may be enough to keep us competitive in the title race. Perhaps Merino could unexpectedly become the solution we lacked up front. Thanks for reading. If you enjoyed the post, please leave a like and share your thoughts below. -- OJ

Odegaard Must Step Up

Merino Delivers

Nwaneri was electric, constantly troubling Leicester’s defence. Trossard improved after shifting left, though he struggled initially as a striker. Sterling offered very little and was replaced by Merino, whose two goals prevented what felt like another frustrating draw.

Can Merino Be the False Nine?

This was only one of the first times Merino has played as a striker, making his impact even more impressive. With only three fit forwards available and Sterling out of form, options are limited. Merino’s physical presence at 6ft 2in and strong aerial ability could make him effective in that role, particularly from crosses.

Trossard remains better suited to the left wing, and Nwaneri thrives on the right, where his creativity drives our attacks. Moving them centrally would reduce attacking balance. That leaves Merino as the most viable solution for now.

He displayed more confidence and clinical instinct than Havertz has shown recently, especially with his header and composed finish. While he has spent most of his career as a midfielder, this unconventional adjustment could prove beneficial.

Conclusion

I’m not entirely convinced this is the long-term answer, but it may be enough to keep us competitive in the title race. Perhaps Merino could unexpectedly become the solution we lacked up front.

Arsenal KNOCKED OUT of Carabao Cup
And to think we really had a chance...

And to think we actually had a chance. *Sigh*. This game happened late at night so I couldn't stay up to watch it, and honestly, I'm glad I didn't. When I saw the 2-0 scoreline the next morning, I didn't even feel disappointed. I read a brief summary, accepted the defeat, and moved on. That too, on the back of a massive 5-1 victory over Manchester City. That's how accustomed I've become to these substandard performances this season. Just when it feels like we're "back in it," something pulls us right back down. Tired Squad I had a feeling this would happen. Going to Newcastle away just days after an intense clash against Manchester City was always going to be difficult. The squad looks exhausted from relentless back-to-back fixtures. Our players aren't superhuman; fatigue was inevitable. This season has exposed how shallow our squad depth truly is. We lost Emile Smith Rowe, Eddie Nketiah, and Reiss Nelson in the summer for modest fees despite them being reliable bench options. Arguably, Nketiah offers similar — if not better — finishing instincts than Havertz has shown recently. To make matters worse, we made zero signings in January despite obvious gaps in the squad. Manchester City added five players. Aston Villa strengthened wisely. Even Tottenham secured Mathys Tel on loan. Meanwhile, we stood still. The Zubimendi signing is promising, but he arrives in the summer. We needed reinforcements immediately — a striker, a winger, perhaps another midfielder. Instead, we strengthened nothing. For a club of Arsenal's stature, that is simply not good enough. In this match, we paid the price. Awful Average Performance No one truly played well, aside from Nwaneri who made a few promising runs. Raya had his first genuinely poor outing in months, making a costly error that allowed Newcastle to double their lead. It was reminiscent of the shaky mistakes he made last season, notably against Tottenham when he nearly squandered a 3-0 lead. To be fair, Raya has been much improved this season, justifying his selection over Ramsdale. His distribution and shot-stopping have largely been solid. This simply felt like an off day. The entire defence struggled, particularly Saliba who failed to contain Isak. The midfield lacked urgency and creativity. The front line, once again, lacked sharpness and attacking hunger. Newcastle were superior and deserved their place in the Carabao Cup final against Liverpool. Credit to them. It will be interesting to see if they can overcome the reigning holders and secure their first major trophy since 1955. Conclusion This performance was disappointing across defence, midfield, and attack. Fatigue plays a role, but ultimately the responsibility falls on the board and management. The squad was not reinforced adequately in either the summer or winter transfer windows for the intensity of English football. Stay tuned for my next report on our trip to Leicester City. If you enjoyed this post, please leave a like and share your thoughts below. -- OJ

The Zubimendi signing is promising, but he arrives in the summer. We needed reinforcements immediately — a striker, a winger, perhaps another midfielder. Instead, we strengthened nothing. For a club of Arsenal’s stature, that is simply not good enough.

In this match, we paid the price.

Awful Average Performance

No one truly played well, aside from Nwaneri who made a few promising runs. Raya had his first genuinely poor outing in months, making a costly error that allowed Newcastle to double their lead. It was reminiscent of the shaky mistakes he made last season, notably against Tottenham when he nearly squandered a 3-0 lead.

To be fair, Raya has been much improved this season, justifying his selection over Ramsdale. His distribution and shot-stopping have largely been solid. This simply felt like an off day.

The entire defence struggled, particularly Saliba who failed to contain Isak. The midfield lacked urgency and creativity. The front line, once again, lacked sharpness and attacking hunger.

Newcastle were superior and deserved their place in the Carabao Cup final against Liverpool. Credit to them. It will be interesting to see if they can overcome the reigning holders and secure their first major trophy since 1955.

Conclusion

This performance was disappointing across defence, midfield, and attack. Fatigue plays a role, but ultimately the responsibility falls on the board and management. The squad was not reinforced adequately in either the summer or winter transfer windows for the intensity of English football.

Game Review: Arsenal vs Aston Villa
Arsenal drew 2-2 to Villa after beating 2-0 up...

Yesterday, we drew 2-2 with Aston Villa at home, marking our 8th draw of the season after bottling a 2-0 lead just before the hour mark. With Darwin Nunez's stoppage-time brace securing Liverpool a 2-0 win against Brentford, Arsenal are once again 6 points behind Liverpool, who still have a game in hand. Arsenal dominated the first half and took the lead as Martinelli converted a cross from Trossard that Martinez failed to clear in time. Ten minutes after the break, Havertz doubled the lead by tapping in another Trossard cross, scoring for the first time since 28th December against Ipswich Town. However, Villa struck twice in eight minutes on the break to level the game. Arsenal pushed late, with a Merino goal disallowed by VAR for a Havertz handball. Minutes later, Merino hit the post and Trossard saw his rebound saved. In the dying moments, Timber slipped Trossard through 1v1 with Martinez, but his shot went narrowly wide. The game ended as a frustrating 2-2 draw. Are Defensive Injuries to Blame? We were cruising at 2-0 before conceding twice in eight minutes. The defence takes significant responsibility. Saliba suffered a hamstring injury which Arteta was "very worried" about, suggesting a potential absence. Timber replaced him alongside Gabriel and performed reasonably well. With Ben White, Tomiyasu, and Calafiori unavailable, and Zinchenko and Kiwior unreliable at right back, Arteta kept Partey at right back. Unfortunately, Partey struggled defensively. He misthrew a ball that gifted Watkins a chance and later failed to track him for the equaliser. Timber or White would likely have handled it better. Myles Lewis-Skelly impressed again with maturity beyond his years. Against a strong Villa side featuring Morgan Rogers, he showed composure and discipline. Still, conceding twice after dominating remains unacceptable. Midfield Imbalance Declan Rice controlled the midfield much like he did against Tottenham, driving forward and setting tempo. However, Odegaard and Merino failed to support him. Odegaard has not returned to his innovative best since his ankle injury, worsened by Saka's absence. His creative spark feels diminished. As captain, he must rediscover that attacking sharpness. Merino contributed little for most of the match. Though strong aerially, his touches are limited and his influence minimal. His late disallowed goal and shot off the post were promising, but overall he remains more suited to a bench role, especially once Zubimendi joins. Attacking Improvements Trossard stepped up significantly. He delivered two excellent assists and was dangerous throughout, though he missed the late winner. Hopefully, this signals a return to form. Martinelli showed improvement on the right flank and made intelligent runs, including the opener. A positive sign. Havertz scored but remained slow and ineffective overall. While the goal may boost confidence, we still need a true striker. Reports suggest RB Leipzig may accept a 70 million Euro bid for Benjamin Sesko, which could greatly strengthen our attack. Conclusion We bottled a 2-0 lead and dropped crucial points. Defensive instability following Saliba's injury hurt us. Rice performed strongly, Trossard improved, but Odegaard must step up and Havertz remains inconsistent. With Wolves away next, we desperately need stability and momentum. If you enjoyed this post, please like and share it. Leave your thoughts below -- I'm open to all feedback. Stay tuned for my review of our Wolves fixture. COYG. --- Om Jhamvar

Gabriel also caused problems from corners and took a strong header that deflected into the goal.

It was another excellent performance from the back five.

Strong Midfield

Declan Rice delivered a remarkable performance. He started slowly but grew into the game, making powerful dribbles through the final third and controlling midfield with authority. Odegaard showed his usual elegance in movement and link-up play, though he still seems slightly below his peak level since Saka’s hamstring injury. Partey gave the ball away a couple of times but still performed adequately overall.

Lingering Attacking Issues

Trossard scored well, but he was the only forward who truly impressed. Sterling was too passive and failed to make the most of his first Premier League start this season. He was replaced by Martinelli at the hour mark, but Martinelli is less effective on the right than on his natural left side. Nwaneri is expected to return later this month and has been the most capable option in Saka’s absence, so his return could be crucial. Havertz worked tirelessly but once again lacked clinical finishing.

All in all, it was another trademark Arsenal performance: fantastic defence, strong midfield control, promising build-up play, but poor finishing. On the bright side, we won a London derby and gained two crucial points on Liverpool.

Confirmation bias: We sure love ourselves!
Confirmation bias is something that affects us all...

I have recently been reading a book called 'The Art of Thinking Clearly' by Rolf Dobelli. It explores cognitive biases, which are systematic deviations from rational thinking. They are tricks our brain plays on us while thinking to process information quickly. Unfortunately, they can result in us making errors in how we make decisions or what we think about the world around us.

One prominent example of this is the 'confirmation bias', in which we look for articles, videos, or other sources of information that support and reinforce our viewpoints and ideologies. For instance, if you are, say, a Ronaldo fan (like me!), you will more likely read about Ronaldo's struggles and achievements and why he is the best footballer ever, rather than focusing on Messi's awards and accomplishments.

In short, confirmation bias is when our brain filters out novel information that contrasts with our existing beliefs (also called 'disconfirming evidence') while keeping information that coincides with our beliefs ('confirming evidence').

We see this phenomenon in various locations. Take, for example, the business world, where a company implements a new strategy to improve it. Any signs suggesting that the strategy has been successful are embraced with wide-open arms, while opposite scenarios are labeled as 'exceptional' cases and ignored. In retrospect, this can cause a business to overlook certain aspects of its operations, significantly affecting its future performance.

In politics, political news is often biased towards one side or another, so when picking a particular viewpoint, people may only follow news sites that portray their preferred party as the 'good' side. This instance of confirmation bias can result in polarization and the citizens of one party simply refusing to listen to the other side, especially if they are part of a large political community with similar beliefs.

In the world of business journalism, a writer will construct a simple theory and find two or three pieces of 'evidence', like 'Apple is an excellent company because it fosters an innovative workplace'. The writer will go on to state several other companies that nurture innovation and how it has contributed to their success. It is important to note here that the writer might be correct in his predictions, but the point is that the writer is very unlikely to search for instances that disprove the writer's theory. This includes cases where companies achieved notoriety and publicity without harboring innovation or nurtured innovation but failed nonetheless. There must be various examples featuring both possibilities, but the writer chooses to ignore them since they would spoil the story.

Astrology functions on similar principles: utterly vague horoscopes like 'Do not try to always be perfect' can be applied to hundreds of millions of lives worldwide, irrespective of their birthday. What's more, general statements such as 'Health is good' apply to practically every one of the eight billion human beings on this planet. This one in particular is dominant in the animal kingdom as well; in fact, it applies to every living organism. And yet it appears in the horoscope section of my newspaper at least thrice a week.

Economists too have been shown to conjure unclear assertions and predictions with no concrete base, predictions that could apply to a variety of scenarios, though they occur less frequently. Even self-help literature is guilty of this cognitive bias. You will often see commonly-repeated phrases like 'Meditation is the key to happiness' and 'Hard work is the key to success'. Personally, I enjoy meditating and although I do not do it often I find it highly calming. Nonetheless, these books do not talk about the many people who are happy despite not meditating or those who deal with depression and anxiety despite regularly practicing mindfulness, choosing instead to portray meditation as a gateway to utopia.

In short, most people relate better to ideas or notions they agree with or those that align with their core values. Avoiding confirmation bias helps you make more informed and objective decisions. When you're open to evidence that challenges your views, you develop a broader perspective and grow intellectually.

--

In GW 3 of EPL, Arsenal have already dropped points in a 1-1 draw against...

In Gameweek 3 of the Premier League, Arsenal drew 1-1 with Brighton at home. They dominated the first half and created several promising chances, and although they failed to convert all of them, a composed chip from Havertz gave Arsenal a deserved lead going into the break. Just three minutes into the second half, however, Declan Rice — already on a yellow card — was controversially sent off.

Instantly, the entire dynamic of the match shifted. Reduced to ten men, Arsenal struggled to maintain control, and Brighton capitalised as their new signing Joao Pedro equalised. From there, Brighton enjoyed more possession thanks to the numerical advantage, while Arsenal arguably fashioned the clearer chances. In the end, strong defending ensured the points were shared.

There is no doubt that until the 48th minute, Arsenal were in control and that Rice’s dismissal altered the contest. As fans, emotional reactions are inevitable when subjective decisions go against your team. But was it actually the wrong call?

Rice had already been cautioned for a mistimed challenge late in the first half. Shortly after the restart, following a foul decision against him, he lightly tapped the ball away as Brighton prepared to take a quick free kick. The Brighton player’s follow-through struck Rice’s leg, and in a surprising twist, it was Rice who received a second yellow for delaying the restart.

Under the current laws of the game, deliberately touching the ball to prevent a quick restart is punishable by a caution. By that interpretation, the referee applied the rule correctly. However, football is not played in isolation from context.

The first issue is inconsistency. Earlier in the match, Joao Pedro kicked the ball away after conceding a throw-in, yet faced no sanction. While no Arsenal player was immediately poised to restart play, the action still delayed proceedings. If one incident is ignored and another punished with a second yellow, frustration is understandable.

Secondly, football officiating is inherently contextual. Referees are tasked not only with enforcing written laws but also with managing flow, intent, and proportionality. A minor tap of the ball — while technically delaying play — may not always warrant a dismissal that fundamentally changes a high-stakes Premier League fixture.

This broader issue extends beyond Arsenal. Across the Premier League and Europe’s top competitions, debates about inconsistency and subjectivity persist. VAR was introduced to increase clarity, yet grey areas remain because interpretation cannot be entirely automated.

Ultimately, while the referee followed the letter of the law, the spirit of the game felt disrupted. Arsenal must accept responsibility for not closing out matches when opportunities arise, but it is equally fair to question whether proportionality was applied.

In the end, the draw leaves mixed feelings. Arsenal showed control, resilience, and defensive solidity with ten men, yet the red card defined the narrative. The lesson may not simply be about refereeing — it may be about discipline, game management, and eliminating avoidable risks in pivotal moments.

It's the start of a new year, and Arsenal are facing many issues...

It’s the start of the new year, and there are many things for Arsenal fans to celebrate. We are statistically the team of the year in the Premier League, largely due to our defensive and offensive prowess that emerged from the start of this year. Apart from breaking our own statistical records, we have enhanced the depth of our team with players like Riccardo Calafiori, Raheem Sterling, Mikel Merino, and academy talents such as Myles Lewis Skelly and Ethan Nwaneri. However, we dropped points on a frustratingly high number of occasions – 8 times this season. Although some of these can be attributed to sending-offs – and we have since improved our discipline – we have drawn several games we should have won. Games We Dropped Unnecessary Points In We should have beaten Fulham 2-1 but drew because Martinelli made a schoolboy error of not staying onside (our xG was above 2). We should have easily beaten 15th-place Everton at home, but we succumbed to a goalless draw despite our “dominance”, as Arteta puts it, and an xG above 1. Against Chelsea, we had countless fantastic chances whipped into the box in the final quarter, most notably a 2-on-1 with Trossard, Havertz, and the keeper in the dying seconds of the game. Trossard should have left it for Havertz, who would have had the easiest of tap-ins to secure a significant London derby victory. But as things stand, the opportunity was wasted. For all these games, we had Odegaard, Saka, Rice, Martinelli, Trossard, and Havertz, so they can’t be blamed on injuries. Instead, it was our inability to score goals from adequate chances that ultimately cost us the three points. In my opinion, scoring goals has been our biggest struggle this season – we can create some lovely chances, but we struggle at getting them in the back of the net. Attacking Struggles Two weeks ago, for Sky Sports’ Monday Night Football special, Thierry Henry gave a thorough explanation regarding our struggles in attack. Most notably, he highlighted the lack of willingness to play forward and take risks as he addressed our “predictable” playing style of routinely passing the ball back. What he says is true, and the root cause of it might be Arteta’s defensive mindset that has largely remained the same over the last few seasons. In an attempt to “dominate” the game, we play too cautiously and take too few risks – especially after grabbing the lead. It seems like the players become complacent with a one or two goal advantage. However, as a title contender for the third time in a row, we must transform ourselves into a fearsome attacking side that frightens defences – like the team we were at the start of last year, scoring 4+ goals consistently for several games in a row. One key hindrance was the Odegaard injury, which lasted about two months. His absence and gradual return were peppered with non-appearances from Rice, Havertz, Trossard, Merino, and Saka. This significantly slowed down our attack. To make matters worse, Saka recently underwent surgery and is expected to be out until March, amounting to two months of the football season without him. Unfortunately, even with our entire squad together and uninjured up front, we have struggled to score goals. Our draws against Chelsea, Fulham, and Everton highlighted the lack of attacking flow. One glaring aspect is the absence of a striker. It has become evident that Havertz is not a true No. 9 due to his slow movements and poor touches in the box. He has struggled to score goals, which puts pressure on other players to step up and do his job. When you get paid 275K a week, you just have to do a better job than that. In fairness, Havertz seems to be playing out of position. He looks more comfortable dropping back a little to support the midfield. Perhaps he is better suited for a midfield role than the striker position. Another key issue is our slow, passive build-up which allows the opposing team to regain their defensive composure, making it much harder for us to break through with clinical chances. In addition, we often try to maintain our defensive shape, dominating the pitch in the final third. However, this cautious gameplay leads to a lot of passing around the box, compromising our attacking prospects to nullify counterattacks and maintain possession. We don't make enough runs forward, and it hurts our goalscoring abilities. Furthermore, we have become too predictable on the right. 45% of our final third attacks linger on the right flank, more than any other team in the Premier League. This is largely due to Saka's dribbling skills and his silky link-up play with Odegaard, with Ben White often running down to complement the build-up. Unfortunately, both full-back and right-wing are injured, and Odegaard is underperforming at the moment. In recent games, such as the one against Everton, the Toffees concentrated their defence on the right, making it hard for Saka and Odegaard to break through. Nevertheless, our build-up play has been neglected on the left, affecting our ability to counter from both sides of the pitch. Conclusion All in all, Arsenal need to revamp their attack. Before it costs us the season. Again.

Poor Decisions Lead To 1-1 Draw At Home In Winnable Game Against Man City...

Last season, Arsenal drew 2-2 with Manchester City after leading 2-1 before Trossard’s red card forced us to defend deep with ten men, only for John Stones to equalise in stoppage time. This time, the script flipped. Haaland opened the scoring again, but City protected their lead with an ultra-defensive 5-5-0 shape. Just when it seemed enough, Gabriel Martinelli struck late to level it. A poetic coincidence — yet from Arsenal’s perspective, it felt like two more points dropped. Team Selection Questions Arteta’s lineup raised eyebrows immediately. Merino started as a number ten ahead of Nwaneri. Trossard was preferred over Eze. Against Manchester City, with title implications looming, the setup felt cautious rather than assertive. If Arsenal aim to win the Premier League, the approach must be proactive — aggressive in possession and ruthless in attack. Instead, the starting structure limited creativity. Merino, naturally more defensive, struggled to unlock space. Trossard lacked directness. The issue was not individual effort, but tactical intent. A Pattern Emerging This mirrored the approach at Anfield weeks earlier, where conservative choices allowed Liverpool to dictate rhythm. Beginning high-stakes matches with a safety-first mindset often hands initiative to elite opponents. Arsenal’s recurring issue is converting control into goals. Playing not to lose only amplifies that weakness. Arteta’s substitutions also invited scrutiny. Merino remained until halftime, Trossard until the 80th minute. The urgency to shift momentum felt delayed. While Martinelli’s equaliser showcased resilience, it masked a broader concern about tactical boldness. No More Excuses This summer’s recruitment has strengthened the squad significantly. Defensive depth, midfield reinforcement, and attacking upgrades with Eze, Gyokeres, and Madueke provide genuine firepower. Arsenal now possess the tools required to compete at the highest level. With that depth comes expectation. For three consecutive seasons, Arsenal have been runners-up. The squad is no longer transitional — it is competitive and mature. This may be a defining campaign for Arteta. Big games require conviction, not hesitation. The Mentality Shift Arteta has elevated Arsenal from mid-table instability to consistent title contention. That achievement deserves respect. But to take the final step, the team must approach decisive matches with controlled aggression rather than caution. In my view, a more attacking setup — Nwaneri over Merino, Eze over Trossard — could have tilted the match. Arsenal’s squad is capable of dominating elite opposition. The question now is whether the tactical mindset will match that potential. The pieces are in place. Now it is about belief, boldness, and execution. -- Om Jhamvar

Year In Review: What's Wrong With Arsenal
This season, Arsenal have been rather poor...

With just a fortnight to 2025, 16 Premier League gameweeks have passed. In this timespan, Arsenal have a paltry 50% win rate, having drawn 6, lost 2 and won just 8. This unexpected and underwhelming first half of the season from Arsenal has astounded me. As things stand, they have 30 points and sit 3rd in the Premier League table, 6 points behind Liverpool who have a game in hand and 1 point behind Chelsea who also have a game in hand -- not to mention their extremely appealing run of fixtures for the next 8 games.

In addition, only 3 points behind Arsenal are four-time champions Manchester City, who will play a Manchester derby at home tonight. Therefore, in a worst-case scenario, Arsenal could end up 4 points behind Chelsea, 9 points behind Liverpool — both of whom currently have a higher GD than Arsenal — and level with Manchester City, who are not our main competitors but still worth mentioning.

Ever since Mikel Arteta joined midseason in 2019, following the departure of Aston Villa manager Unai Emery, it seemed as though Arsenal kept progressing and improving in some aspect or the other. The 19/20 season was shaky, but Arteta did join midseason and ultimately beat Chelsea 2-1 to win a record-extending 14th FA Cup.

When the pandemic struck, Arsenal lost several key players and finished 8th for a second successive season, leading to a lot of criticism and doubt. The next season, however, Arsenal bounced back from an extremely poor start to finish 5th, just missing out on the Champions League. In 22/23, Arsenal defied all expectations to lead the Premier League, faltering in the last month but earning a steady 2nd place.

This was a major success for Arteta's project of rebuilding a new, young squad that could challenge for the title. After winning the Community Shield, Arsenal had an even more successful Premier League season, missing out on the title by the finest of margins and reaching the UCL quarter-finals for the first time in 14 years. It was another trophyless season, but to me, the promise of progression justified the silverware scarcity.

Twice we had come second, only bested by the Manchester City juggernaut. Coming into the 24/25 season, I was only praying for a City downfall, which would surely guarantee an Arsenal title for the first time in 21 years... right? However, with the season almost halfway through, I'm not so convinced.

Arsenal have consistently been dropping points this season. It started against Brighton, Manchester City, and Bournemouth due to silly red card challenges, and was exacerbated by a ligament injury that forced team captain Odegaard onto the sidelines for months. His absence only highlighted his importance to the club, so his return, which coincided with other injuries clearing up as well, seemed like a fresh start for Arsenal. They won 3-0, 5-1, and 5-2 against Nottingham Forest, Sporting CP, and West Ham, scoring 13 goals in just a week.

However, back-to-back draws in the Premier League since then have dampened my hopeful spirits of a 10 or 15-game winning streak that would bolster our title challenge as we play catchup with Liverpool. Instead, they dropped 4 points against Fulham and Everton, games we should have won, and have now given Chelsea a chance to outpace them as well.

These draws were unnecessary, and as title contenders, Arsenal should have won them without a second thought. In their 1-1 draw against Fulham, they had an xG above 2 while Fulham's was a trivial 0.16. Fast forward to the Emirates where, in a goalless draw against a team inches away from relegation, it was 1.22 vs 0.09. A stark difference that is not visible in the slightest on the scoresheet.

There are many factors to blame for this slip-up. On one hand, there were the disciplinary issues which have been improved upon and the key injuries that have largely cleared up now. Despite this, Arsenal have not yet returned to their dominant ways.

In my opinion, the team should take most of the blame for this. First and foremost, there is the glaring issue of our squad missing a key striker. Ever since Aubemeyang left in the 2022 winter transfer window due to a fallout with Arteta and poor discipline, Arsenal haven't found a suitable replacement.

Kai Havertz was supposed to fill in the gap but he has been a major disappointment. I like him quite a bit and he is rather hardworking. However, he isn't a true center forward and rarely has the impact required. What Arsenal genuinely need right now is a talented striker who can finish off chances and score goals. From what I've seen in these past few months, we have created brilliant chances, and the xG stats back me up here. However, our finishing has been below standard, and it has cost us crucial points.

In this case, you need a player like Erling Haaland, Alexander Isak, Chris Wood, or Ollie Watkins, who are quick in the penalty area and get the ball in the back of the net. After all, in football, it doesn't matter how fluid your build-up play is if you can't score. It's like doing well in a sprint race but stopping a few feet before the finish line.

I'm not trying to be harsh, but Kai Havertz is slow in the penalty area and does not reliably finish off chances, which to me makes his high work rate irrelevant. He tries and tries, but the Premier League is a results industry, especially when you are a striker and your team is contending for the title.

Gabriel Jesus is even worse: he has yet to register a goal this season in all competitions, despite having countless chances to score a goal. He was good for Arsenal two seasons ago but has completely dropped off since then, and if you ask me, his time in Arsenal is done -- along with Oleksandr Zinchenko, with Calafiori and Myles Lewis-Skelly performing far better at left-back.

Martinelli has also been performing poorly for the past few months and is struggling to create an impact in the game. His inefficiency at times causes Arsenal's build-up to shift to the right, with Saka and Odegaard, destabilizing it and making it harder to break through. How Contemporary Filmmakers Confront Social and Political Issues...

A study published in Social Science Quarterly found that movies with political messages can significantly influence viewers' opinions on social and political issues like healthcare reform, and these effects can last for weeks. This only begins to uncover the impact contemporary filmmakers can have in addressing societal challenges. In this essay, I will explore the cinematic techniques used by modern filmmakers to confront these issues. I will also examine how these techniques are used in different films.

Filmmakers often use emotional appeal, or pathos, to draw viewers into characters' struggles and make complex social issues more relatable. Movies like “Black Panther” and “The Kite Runner” use this emotional connection to highlight the difficulties faced by underrepresented groups, such as African and Muslim communities.

The superhero movie “Black Panther” not only celebrates African heritage but also offers vital representation for African audiences. As Jamil Smith points out in the Times, such representation is scarce in mainstream media, making Black Panther a notable cultural achievement.

Similarly, “The Kite Runner” explores the prejudice faced by Muslim and Asian communities by focusing on the main character, Amir, who grapples with suspicion and bias in the aftermath of the World Trade Center attacks. Through Amir’s relationships and personal struggles, the film shows how these biases impact real lives.

Ultimately, by creating strong emotional connections between viewers and characters, films allow audiences to experience and empathize with the struggles of disadvantaged communities, making social issues like these feel immediate and personal.

Filmmakers also use different narrative styles to address political or social issues differently. Firstly, documentaries use unfiltered footage to present the genuine views of marginalized communities. By avoiding sensationalism, they let ignored voices be heard in the media, encouraging important talks about justice.

In addition, many documentaries display important political events in engaging ways. In “The Big Short”, director Adam McKay uses humor, clear metaphors, varying tones, and fourth wall breaks to explain the 2008 monetary crisis, making complex financial ideas easier for viewers to comprehend.

Furthermore, biopics like “The Iron Lady” use character-driven narratives to convey political issues. This technique allows viewers to connect with the characters and understand their feelings or underlying motivations, making the problems more relatable.

When contemporary filmmakers address critical issues in their films, they incorporate several aspects in the storyline and dialogues. However, they also use subtle techniques like lighting, sound, and editing to reinforce the message they wish to create.

In general, harsh lighting creates discomfort and unease and typically depicts a grim reality whereas soft, natural lighting conveys hope. Silence is frequently used as a metaphor for voicelessness or suppression, making it especially effective in dystopian films like “The Hunger Games”. Moreover, fast-paced editing can create urgency around prominent issues and convey chaos and complexity. On the contrary, long takes and slow pacing can let the viewer dwell on the emotional weight of social problems, giving them time to reflect on it.

In conclusion, bonding viewers and characters while using an appropriate narrative style and tweaking certain technical aspects during filming are effective ways of presenting political and social topics, especially if the character represents a broader societal matter. In conclusion, movies are a powerful way to address social or political issues, and modern-day filmmakers have the potential to influence public opinion significantly.

At Havard YLC, I learn about six common myths regarding leadership...

Here are six common leadership myths I learned at the Harvard YLC. 1. Leadership is Innate Many people believe leaders are born, not made. While some individuals may possess natural traits that support leadership, effective leadership is rarely purely innate. It requires continuous learning, reflection, adaptability, and skill development. Both introverts and extroverts can become impactful leaders if they possess vision and the ability to guide others. Leadership is cultivated over time, not predetermined at birth. 2. You Can’t Be a Leader Leadership is not reserved for CEOs or heads of state. It can exist in classrooms, families, sports teams, and community projects. Leadership begins with initiative and responsibility, not status. Anyone willing to take ownership and guide others toward a shared objective can demonstrate leadership. 3. Leadership Is About Power Power alone does not define leadership. True leadership encompasses vision, communication, integrity, empowerment, and ethical decision-making. Influential figures such as Nelson Mandela demonstrated that empowering others often creates more sustainable impact than exerting authority. Leadership is about influence, not dominance. 4. Leaders Must Have Titles Titles do not create leaders; leaders give meaning to titles. Many impactful individuals operate without formal recognition, yet they inspire, organize, and elevate those around them. Authority may come with a title, but leadership comes from action and credibility. 5. Leaders Control Outcomes Leaders can shape direction and strategy, but outcomes ultimately depend on collective effort. Success is rarely the product of one individual. Effective leaders align teams, foster collaboration, and create environments where everyone contributes meaningfully. 6. Leaders Are Always Obvious Not all leaders are visible or outspoken. Some lead quietly through consistency, support, and example. Leadership styles vary widely, making it difficult to identify leadership based solely on personality. Impact, not volume, is what truly defines a leader. -- Om

Here are six common leadership myths I learned at the Harvard YLC.

1. Leadership is Innate

Many people believe leaders are born, not made. While some individuals may possess natural traits that support leadership, effective leadership is rarely purely innate. It requires continuous learning, reflection, adaptability, and skill development. Both introverts and extroverts can become impactful leaders if they possess vision and the ability to guide others. Leadership is cultivated over time, not predetermined at birth.

2. You Can’t Be a Leader

Leadership is not reserved for CEOs or heads of state. It can exist in classrooms, families, sports teams, and community projects. Leadership begins with initiative and responsibility, not status. Anyone willing to take ownership and guide others toward a shared objective can demonstrate leadership.

3. Leadership Is About Power

Power alone does not define leadership. True leadership encompasses vision, communication, integrity, empowerment, and ethical decision-making. Influential figures such as Nelson Mandela demonstrated that empowering others often creates more sustainable impact than exerting authority. Leadership is about influence, not dominance.

4. Leaders Must Have Titles

Titles do not create leaders; leaders give meaning to titles. Many impactful individuals operate without formal recognition, yet they inspire, organize, and elevate those around them. Authority may come with a title, but leadership comes from action and credibility.

5. Leaders Control Outcomes

Leaders can shape direction and strategy, but outcomes ultimately depend on collective effort. Success is rarely the product of one individual. Effective leaders align teams, foster collaboration, and create environments where everyone contributes meaningfully.

6. Leaders Are Always Obvious

Not all leaders are visible or outspoken. Some lead quietly through consistency, support, and example. Leadership styles vary widely, making it difficult to identify leadership based solely on personality. Impact, not volume, is what truly defines a leader.

Today was my first session at the Harvard YLC, a 5 day course where Harvard students come to teach us...

Today, I attended the first session of the five-day Harvard YLC (Youth Lead the Change) leadership conference, in which Harvard students came to India to teach us about leadership. The opening hour was dedicated to exploring and defining the broad concept of leadership, and through discussion we identified several core characteristics and responsibilities that effective leaders should embody. The Role of a Leader A leader’s responsibility is not to control, but to guide and elevate a team. That includes maintaining discipline and direction, ensuring the group remains aligned with its objectives, and creating a culture of accountability. Leaders must encourage and motivate their team to perform at their highest potential. They should accept responsibility for collective outcomes, ensure every voice is heard, and stand up for their team when necessary. Strong leadership also demands persistence, resilience, and the willingness to take calculated risks. Decisions should be rooted in logic, reasoning, and evidence rather than impulse or emotion. At the same time, leaders must understand the needs and aspirations of their team members, ensuring that individuals feel supported and comfortable — because empowered individuals perform at their best. These qualities strengthen team dynamics, improve collaboration, and increase the likelihood of meaningful success. Ultimately, the most important realization from today’s session was this: the true purpose of leadership is not to exert control, but to guide, inspire, and empower others toward a shared vision. -- Om

GYOKERES TO ARSENAL, HERE WE GO!!!
Our number nine is here at last! ...

We’ve known it for over a month — and now it’s official. Gyokeres to Arsenal, HERE WE GO. After three seasons without a true successor to Aubameyang, Arsenal have finally signed a recognised, ruthless number nine. Having finished second in the Premier League three consecutive times, the message was clear: we needed a striker to push us over the line. Elite Numbers in Portugal The 27-year-old Swedish international arrives after a staggering spell at Sporting CP, scoring 97 goals in 102 matches across two seasons and leading them to back-to-back league titles. His explosive pace, intelligent movement, physical dominance, and clinical finishing made him one of Europe’s most productive forwards. He even recorded a hat-trick against Manchester City — a statement performance that Arsenal fans hope he can replicate at the Emirates. The Missing Piece Last season exposed our biggest flaw. We dominated possession, created high-quality chances, but lacked a reliable finisher. Too many draws, too many narrow margins. Injuries to Jesus and inconsistent performances from Havertz only highlighted the void. Even Merino’s temporary shift into a false nine role couldn’t fully address the problem. This signing represents intent. The Transfer Context Arsenal paid £63 million — a figure that feels reasonable in today’s market. Alternative targets included Benjamin Sesko, Victor Osimhen, and Alexander Isak. Sesko offers long-term upside but required patience and complex negotiations. Osimhen and Isak are proven at the highest levels but would have cost well over £100 million, with limited indications of availability. Gyokeres may not yet be Premier League-proven, but he arrives in peak physical condition and at the height of his confidence. The Pressure Factor The expectations are immense. He joins a squad desperate for silverware and a fanbase craving that final leap. The Portuguese league differs in tempo and physical intensity, so adaptation will be crucial. Missing portions of preseason could slow integration, and Arsenal’s season opener at Old Trafford offers little easing-in period. Early rhythm will matter. A New Chapter Despite the pressure, excitement outweighs doubt. Arsenal finally possess a striker built to convert the chances our system consistently produces. Whether he becomes the next iconic number nine or forges his own identity, this transfer signals ambition. If Gyokeres settles quickly and delivers consistently, this could be the missing piece in a long-awaited title-winning puzzle. A new chapter begins. -- Om Jhamvar

Most people believe that only massive actions can transform their lives, but...

Most people believe that only massive actions can transform their lives. But #1 New York Times bestselling author James Clear presents a different perspective in his book Atomic Habits, which I recently finished reading. Through four simple yet powerful laws, he demonstrates how small, consistent improvements compound into extraordinary results. This book genuinely inspired me to begin writing blogs (more on that soon), and I believe it is a must-read for anyone serious about personal growth. The Power of Small Habits Clear introduces practical techniques such as Habit Stacking, the Goldilocks Rule, and the Two-Minute Rule — a deceptively simple strategy that lowers resistance and makes consistency easier. These frameworks show how designing systems, rather than chasing motivation, is the key to long-term change. He reinforces these ideas with compelling real-world examples, most notably the transformation of British Cycling, illustrating how marginal gains can lead to elite performance. Throughout the book, he explains the psychology and behavioral science behind habit formation in clear, jargon-free language, making complex ideas accessible and actionable. Breaking Bad Habits Too Beyond building good habits, Clear also inverts the four laws to help readers dismantle negative patterns. Whether it relates to studying, fitness, work, or parenting, he provides practical, relatable scenarios that can be applied immediately. Over the next few weeks, I plan to share chapter-by-chapter summaries and reflections, highlighting key insights and applications. If you’re interested, you can explore more of James Clear’s work and resources at jamesclear.com. Thanks for reading! -- Om

Beyond The Badge: A Tribute To Jota
As an Arsenal fan, I never thought much about Diogo...

As an Arsenal fan, I never thought too much about Diogo Jota. He was simply part of the opposition -- dangerous, clinical, always seeming to score when you least wanted him to. I didn’t follow his journey closely or celebrate his milestones. But today, that changed. A Life Cut Short Waking up to the news that Diogo Jota, just 28 years old, had died in a car accident alongside his brother André stopped me in my tracks. Suddenly, he wasn’t just a rival forward. He was a husband who had recently married his childhood partner, a father of three young children, a man who had just celebrated major triumphs for club and country. A life full of momentum and promise, ended far too soon. Reports stated that his vehicle suffered a tyre blowout near Spain, caught fire, and within moments everything was gone. Just days earlier he was celebrating one of the happiest periods of his life. Now the world reads tributes. It feels unreal. More Than a Rival Looking back, Jota accomplished far more than I had acknowledged. Sixty-five goals for Liverpool. Multiple Premier League titles. Key domestic contributions. Forty-nine caps for Portugal and two Nations League triumphs. Yet beyond the numbers was something deeper. After revisiting his performances, I saw the intensity in his movement, the sharp pressing, the energy in his celebrations. There was humility and hunger in how he played. It was clear he loved the game. Perspective Beyond Rivalry This moment reminds us how easy it is to reduce people to shirts and rivalries. Football can make us tribal, but events like this strip it back to something human. Behind every opponent is a life, a family, a story still being written. Today, I am not reflecting on Diogo Jota the Liverpool forward. I am reflecting on Diogo Jota the person. A vibrant life taken too early, with so much still to give both on and off the pitch. Rest in peace, Jota. The game feels quieter without you. -- Om Jhamvar

A Life Cut Short

Waking up to the news that Diogo Jota, just 28 years old, had died in a car accident alongside his brother André stopped me in my tracks. Suddenly, he wasn’t just a rival forward. He was a husband who had recently married his childhood partner, a father of three young children, a man who had just celebrated major triumphs for club and country. Facing 15-time Champions League winners Real Madrid without Gabriel felt daunting. With Kiwior expected to face Bellingham, Vinicius Jr, and Mbappe, confidence was limited.

Total Control

Declan Rice’s Historic Night

Madueke To Arsenal, ANOTHER CHELSEA PLAYER?
Arsenal have made headlines again, this time by signing...

Arsenal have made headlines once again — this time with a bold £52 million move for Noni Madueke. The signing has sparked widespread debate. Is this Arteta and Berta identifying untapped potential, or an expensive gamble on inconsistency? Initial Reaction As an Arsenal fan, I can confidently say the fanbase has been craving decisive transfer action after quiet windows last season. This summer, however, has been transformative. Kepa, Norgaard, Zubimendi, Madueke, Gyokeres, and Mosquera have all joined, with Eze still linked. On paper, this looks like one of the most ambitious windows in recent memory. Still, the Madueke fee raised eyebrows. At £52 million for a player expected primarily to rotate with Saka, the valuation felt steep. Some fans reacted emotionally, even crossing moral boundaries by vandalising murals and directing abuse toward the player’s family — behaviour that is completely unjustifiable. Breaking Down the Player Madueke, 23, joined Chelsea for £28.5 million in January 2023 and now leaves for nearly double that fee. He is known for pace, direct dribbling, and confidence in one-versus-one situations. Statistically, his dribble volume compares favourably with Saka, but he trails in chance creation metrics such as expected assists and big chances created. His likely role is squad reinforcement — crucial given how injuries exposed our lack of depth last season. Providing rotation for Saka reduces overreliance and injury risk, while potentially introducing internal competition. The Price Question The inflated price tag inevitably increases scrutiny. Madueke will carry pressure, as will the recruitment team. However, Arteta has a history of unpopular decisions that ultimately proved correct: prioritising Raya, phasing out Aubameyang, and reshaping the squad by moving on high-wage veterans. Raya’s distribution elevated our build-up structure and he secured consecutive Golden Glove awards. Squad discipline improved post-Aubameyang. Strategic turnover allowed young core players like Saka and Saliba to flourish. The broader lesson is that internal strategic decisions often look different from the outside. Backing the Process Arteta and Berta understand the squad’s tactical and psychological needs better than external observers. While doubts about value are valid, trust in the long-term project remains essential. Whether supporters approve or not, Madueke is now an Arsenal player. The club benefits most if he succeeds. Backing him is not blind optimism — it is alignment with the team’s interests. I remain cautiously supportive. I hope he proves me wrong in the best possible way. -- Om Jhamvar

On the back of our best game in UCL history...

A few days back, Arsenal drew 1-1 with Manchester United after beating PSV 7-1 in their biggest Champions League away victory in history. Many expected a dominant Arsenal win at Old Trafford. Strangely, I expected a draw. After this season's repeated false hope, I refused to overhype a six-goal win without context. There were three reasons for that PSV demolition: PSV were out of form; their manager made serious tactical errors by spreading the midfield wide, leaving space between the lines; and Arsenal capitalised clinically on those mistakes. Fun fact: Arsenal's xG in that 7-1 win was only 1.96. Compare that to approximately 3.50 xG across two legs against Newcastle in the Carabao Cup, where we failed to score. The difference? Clinical finishing. Odegaard controlled that PSV game brilliantly, and even if Lewis-Skelly had been sent off, Arsenal likely still would have won because PSV refused to use a low block. United's Low Block Problem Manchester United did the opposite. They parked the bus. Arsenal, despite confidence from Eindhoven, lacked the attacking quality to break down a compact defence. Amorim's side were content to concede possession because Arsenal could not convert it into clear chances. Bruno Fernandes scored a superb free-kick just before half-time. Rice equalised in the second half to salvage a point. It was a familiar pattern: dominate possession, lack a clinical forward line, concede first, then scramble for a result. Defensive Concerns Raya reacted slowly to the free-kick but redeemed himself with crucial saves. United created significant chances late on, and without Raya's interventions Arsenal likely would have lost. The defence was largely stable but allowed too many dangerous moments. Against elite attackers like Vinicius, Jude, or Mbappe, such lapses could prove costly. Title Realities The Premier League title was effectively gone weeks ago. Now it is only a matter of when Liverpool confirm it. Arsenal's only realistic silverware chance remains the Champions League. The 2-2 draw with PSV in the return leg was largely uneventful, aside from Sterling's two goal involvements. Unfortunately, his suspension means he misses the first leg against Madrid. Looking Ahead to Real Madrid Arsenal face reigning European champions Real Madrid in the quarter-finals. It will require a significant step-up in performance to progress. Interestingly, I slightly prefer facing Madrid over Atletico. Atletico's low block would suffocate our attack, whereas Madrid's more open style may allow Arsenal scoring opportunities. The real test will be our defence. There are rumours about Saka potentially returning for the Madrid tie, but given how rusty Ben White looked upon return, I remain doubtful. Next up is a London derby against Chelsea at the Emirates. What are your thoughts? Let me know below. -- OJ

To all my amazing football-loving readers,

The most predictable injury in the Premier League has finally happened.

Kai Havertz will miss the rest of the season due to a hamstring injury sustained during training in Dubai. Ironically, a warm-weather camp intended to boost fitness ended the season of our only senior centre-forward.

Injury Crisis Deepens

This adds to Jesus’s ACL injury suffered in the FA Cup defeat to Manchester United. Saka is out until at least April, potentially longer. Martinelli is sidelined for nearly a month. In total, four senior forwards are injured, three long-term.

We were already struggling for goals even when Havertz and Martinelli were fit. Now the likely forward line consists of Nwaneri, Trossard, and Merino as a makeshift striker. Sterling offers little impact, and depth is practically nonexistent.

A Preventable Situation

To some extent, this is misfortune. But it was entirely foreseeable. Arsenal entered January needing at least one striker, arguably two, even before further injuries struck. Yet we made zero signings.

This came after humiliating exits from domestic competitions and dropped points in crucial matches. Despite clear warning signs, the board chose inaction.

January Window Failure

Loan options such as Kolo Muani, Asensio, and Rashford were available. Depth could have been strengthened without major financial strain. Even recalling loaned players like Fabio Vieira would have added cover. Instead, the club gambled on internal solutions.

Havertz played virtually every match, pressing relentlessly as a false nine following a demanding summer tournament. An injury felt inevitable. Now, just after the window closed, it has arrived.

Accountability

Arteta initially resisted external signings, preferring internal options, but later acknowledged the need for reinforcements. While he shares some responsibility, the greater blame lies with the board and management.

The lack of urgency suggests satisfaction with Champions League qualification rather than genuine ambition for major trophies.

Conclusion

This January window may ultimately define our season. The damage is done. The best we can hope for now is that Merino adapts successfully or that the club corrects course in the summer.

If similar mistakes persist, deeper structural changes may be necessary.

This is my research from my 5th grade deforestation debate!

Deforestation has played a significant role in human development and civilization. While it carries a negative reputation due to environmental damage often caused by greed, poor agricultural practices, and weak governance, it is important to acknowledge that, when conducted with proper planning and oversight, deforestation can generate certain economic and social benefits.

## 1. Employment and Livelihood

Deforestation provides employment opportunities for millions of people worldwide. In many developing regions, forest clearing enables farming, cattle grazing, and small-scale businesses that sustain families. Over 250 million people live in forest and savannah regions where land conversion is closely tied to survival. For economically disadvantaged communities, these activities often represent a primary source of income and food security.

## 2. Housing and Urban Development

As global populations expand — with projections nearing 10 billion — pressure on land resources intensifies. Deforestation can create space for housing, infrastructure, and urban expansion. In overpopulated regions facing housing shortages, cleared land may provide room for communities, businesses, and public facilities, potentially contributing to economic growth.

## 3. Raw Materials for Production

Forests supply valuable raw materials such as timber and palm oil. Wood is essential for construction, furniture, and paper products, while palm oil is widely used in food, cosmetics, and household items. When combined with responsible reforestation practices, controlled land conversion can support sustainable resource cycles.

## 4. Industrialization and Economic Activity

Opening land for factories, roads, and businesses can stimulate industrial development. Economic growth generated through employment increases consumer spending, which circulates through local economies — a multiplier effect that boosts overall economic activity. Additionally, tax revenues from such activities can fund public services and infrastructure like roads, bridges, and welfare programs.

## 5. Agricultural Expansion and Food Supply

Converting forest land into agricultural land can increase food production. Crops, livestock grazing, and plantations can help address food security concerns, particularly in regions facing rapid population growth. Greater agricultural output may reduce scarcity and improve economic stability in certain communities.

## 6. Access to Natural Resources

Some forests sit above valuable underground resources such as oil, natural gas, coal, and minerals. Extracting these resources can generate significant economic returns and supply materials used in energy production, manufacturing, and infrastructure development.

## 7. Scientific Discovery

In some cases, exploration in forested regions has led to the discovery of new species and expanded scientific understanding. Research into newly identified organisms may contribute to ecological knowledge and even medical advancements.

While these advantages exist, the broader impact of deforestation depends entirely on how responsibly it is managed. Without sustainable planning, oversight, and reforestation efforts, the environmental costs — including biodiversity loss, climate change acceleration, and ecosystem disruption — can outweigh economic gains. The true challenge lies not in whether deforestation can produce benefits, but in whether it can be balanced responsibly with long-term environmental sustainability.

--
